replies.author: @gunslingersymphony5015
replies.text:
​​@ThatGuy-tx4vm I suppose I could have been clearer.

TL;DR: GL obviously didn't have thick enough skin; but, having experienced it all firsthand, I was simply commenting that it wouldn't kill us to ease up and trust the artist. God knows everyone else involved already has their own ideas about someone else's vision, ya know?

I didn't intend to imply that GL or any other artist (or person, for that matter) should be free from any criticism, ever. That kind of thinking is what got us into this mess.

A fair-minded, level headed approach and reaction to said criticism, on both sides, is also necessary for productive development, both as an artist and a person. In other words: if you want to put your creation into the world, you need to have thick skin.

GL caved the second people whined about Jar Jar. This was, of course, literally on the way out of the theater, opening day of E1 according to my recollection of listening to everyone complain. He then completely changed the story (according to accounts I read years ago and verified a few years later, but haven't since), but decided to be (hilariously) petty by still keeping him front and center. Not the most mature way to handle it, but I definitely remember he was already talking about selling then, so I'll take it.

I just wish we could have a little faith in the creators we love, especially when they've earned it with a trilogy that has stood the test of time already.

replies.author: @ThatGuy-tx4vm
replies.text: ​@gunslingersymphony5015hmmm. I think you are right about Lucas caving in immediately but i also think that it is the faith, reverence and his perception of it that led to him selling. Consider that from 1983 till 1999 he could do no wrong. Until episode 1 was released everyone revered Lucas. I bet that got to his head at some point and he took more liberties because of it... And then he worked without a filter for the prequels and it led to hits and misses... The fans trusted him completely so they were surprised by the errors he made in the prequels(even though the story holds up regardless). He was also surprised at the repsonse he got considering that he believed himself to be universally loved instead of accepting that star wars was a result of a mix of ideas and not him being told yes at every point. If he wasn't revered like a faultless messiah before the prequels then he would not have been disappointed and sold after episode 3. He should have also known what the fans actually thought based on his rating of the past movies. He regarded episode 5 as the worst in the trilogy(it had the most involvement from other people)... The fans on the other hand regarded 5 as the best of the entire saga. Lucas clearly believed his own hype for a while but thankfully he still loved the IP and story

@NetWebUser i actually had this realization the other day. I realized that I actually didn’t hate the force awakens. I watched it in theaters and I was bored half way through because it was just a crappy imitation of a new hope. I enjoyed it as a Star Wars movie but was let down by it not being exciting and original stuff that would make me want to go back and watch it again. However it was when the last Jedi came out that I shifted towards being upset and rejecting everything. I was so apathetic and frustrated that when the rise of skywalker came out I waited to watch it at home instead of in theatres. I’m happy I did as 20 minutes in I found myself rolling my eyes and watching it only to see how bad it would get. 

The crazy part is I am an original Star Wars fan. I was a huge fan before the prequels as my dad’s best friend was the biggest Star Wars nerd in the world. He had a basement full of models he had tediously built and glued together. He even had a death star he hung from the ceiling with x wings, tie fighters, imperial star destroyers and the millennium falcon to make it look like they were in the middle of the battle from the first movie. 

I got to meet Peter Mayhew who was the man who played Chewbacca. I got to meet Anthony Daniel’s that played C-3PO and this is before the phantom menace even came out. 

I remember going to opening night of the phantom menace dressed as a Jedi and in a packed theater with excited fans only for us all to leave the theatre disappointed but even as a kid excited seeing a new light saber battle. 

I remember seeing attack of the clones in a half filled theater and feeling sad that the excitement was gone. Then being in a completely empty theater for revenge of the sith. At one point a couple came in 20 minutes late and watched for an hour only to leave and never come back. I felt actual sadness and disappointment because I had read the books and understood the backstory. I enjoyed the prequels because I knew this was the story it was going to tell. I went through high school being made fun of because only me and my friend liked the prequels and said they would be remembered as being good. 

I think the sequels represented for me the most jarring thing where I wasn’t even upset, I was just watching the culmination of what happens when the story is not the focus of the movie. 

There’s an infamous meme of George Lucas surrounded by dozens of models in 1983 while sitting in front of a green screen by himself in 2005. 

The magic of the original Star Wars was that the story had to be compelling and interesting and the majority of the movie was filmed in a desert with actors wearing simple costumes and drawing you in with the plot and dialogue. The special effects were only a small portion of the movie and they were a reward for depicting the story that had been created during the entire movie. 

There’s worst moment from the sequels was when at one point we believe Chewbacca has been killed as a ship explodes only to have him shown as being alive in the very next scene. There’s worst moment original Star Wars movies depicted our favorite character Han Solo as possibly being dead at the very end of the empire strikes back and original fans had to wait 3 years to be relieved that he wasn’t dead and was frozen and saved. That’s how you make a compelling story. That’s how you keep your audience invested. People need to worry that their favorite characters can die. Obi wan dies in the first movie which as a 7 year old boy watching the rerelease in theaters in Chicago was a crushing moment. Having someone who I loved in the movie die was what made the movie even better. It didn’t make me hate Star Wars. It made me love it for being so invested in their characters.

replies.author: @PsychedeliKompot
replies.text:
If you want to understand Katee Sackhoff, you need to watch Battlestar Galactica, where in many ways she basically portrayed herself. When she played Kara Thrace "Starbuck", that was a character she could relate to. A real human in many ways. A very flawed person with self destructive tendencies and a particular set of ways to deal with issues that often didn't result in the desired outcome for her. She was able to identify with that to quite a degree, likely by having been through similar struggles at points in her life prior.

Bo Katan in The Clone Wars was not quite the same, but still flawed enough and at least somewhat vulnerable. It also would've helped that Bo Katan obviously was just a voice acting role - she didn't need to actually embody the character on camera. But when Bo Katan was changed from a flawed leader into a flawless girlboss on screen AND she had to embody that and portray it, it broke her. This was not a character she could identify with in any way, not a role she could get into. 

This, as an actress, absolutely would cast doubts upon your own ability to perform your job. I hope she is doing better now, because ultimatel it never was her fault. She did well when Bo Katan was just a voice acting gig, and she did the best on Mando Season 3 that one could expect given the writing her character was given.

I think she also felt bad about her character being brought into the franchise that way and usurping it without necessarily having "earned" that position. Similar to how Mando usurped Boba Fett in the BOBF series - with the exception that Pedro Pascal didn't feel bad about it in the slightest, while Katee seems to have.

I'd love to see her on screen as Starbuck again. That was the role she excelled at. But Battlestar Galactica wrapped up so neatly and well that ... what possibility is there of actually seeing her in that role again? She is too old now to portray the character in a movie set during the series, and with the character dead at the end of the series, how could she return to the role to portray an older version of it?

It's a crime that Disney can basically just *break* an actor/actress in the name of making a reference the audience will get. 99.9% of Star Wars' problem as a story is that it's become so incestuously self referential there's no longer a good entry point. Not even the original trilogy works as an entry point to the series anymore, it's that bad.

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
Nope. Anyone who looks at it in a historical context in 100 years will call it a surprise success that induced lots of changes in how films were made, marketed, and merchandised, that was sold off after 30 years of diminishing returns that was unlikely to return to prominence after its sale. The first two entries in the saga were wonderful, the third was still good, but not as good, and the majority of things it spawned outside of the three films throughout the 1980s were mediocre at best. Look at what was released between 1977 and 1990 outside of the OT: The Holiday Special, the Marvel comics series, the three Han Solo Adventures novels, the three Lando Calrissian Adventures, _Splinter of the Mind's Eye,_ the short-lived cartoon series _Droids_ and _Ewoks,_ the two Ewok Adventure films, and the West End Games Star Wars RPG. The books are all relatively obscure to anyone who isn't a pretty big SW fan, the cartoons were pretty bad even by the standards of early '80s Saturday morning cartoons, and the Ewok Adventure films were nearly as bad as the Holiday Special. The one bright spot was the WEG RPG, which was pretty well loved by everyone who played it, but it was never widely played. It wasn't until 1991 with the publication of _Heir to the Empire_ that Star Wars really came back to any kind of prominence, and the only reason Lucas made the Prequels was due to the success of the CGI in _Jurassic Park._ No matter how much individual fans may enjoy the Prequels, and I am absolutely a fan who enjoys them, they are objectively worse than the OT, no matter how much money they made. Furthermore, while I personally love the majority of the EU novels, they are very hit or miss. Some of them are fantastic, others are absolutely horrendous, and the majority fall somewhere in the fair to slightly above average range. Lucasfilm continued to make money not because the content they were releasing was as good as the original films, but because by the early 2000s the kids who fell in love with the films in the early '80s were finally making enough money to allow them to invest in the franchise that they loved as kids. 

Both Lucas and Disney _COULD_ have done things to keep the franchise relevant, but they both failed to do so.

replies.text:
@higherground9888 
*" Where the Movies and books sometimes stumbled, the GAMES did not."*
If you'd actually read and understood my comment, you'd have realized I limited the majority of my comment to the things created between 1977 and 1990 outside of the original films. There were a total of eight Star Wars video games released in the '80s, and the majority of them were simply adaptations of the films or parts of the films. 

*"SW became a game driven franchise. And rather than embrace that, Disney shuttered LucasArts."*
Except it didn't become a game driven franchise. The best estimate available are that around 90 million Star Wars games were sold by 2020. Since LucasArts was shut down in April 2013, that means many of those sales were for games _not produced by LucasArts._ By 2016, there were more than 100 million total book sales. There have absolutely been more Star Wars books sold than video games, and the books did far more to drive the continued interest in Star Wars than the games.

author: @AllardRT
text:
I was a Star Wars fan. I spent an ungodly amount of hours perfecting my Russian translation of Knights of the Old Republic (still got to tweak a few things here and there). I got all of the movies, all six of them on DVD and blu-ray. I read dozens of Expanded Universe books. Played dozens of Star Wars video games. Read quite a few comics.

I didn't watch the trailer. 

I said in the beginning that I was a Star Wars fan. I guess I still am. But I'm a fan of the pre-Disney Star Wars. The Lucas era Star Wars. I was one of the prequel kids. I was twelve years old when Revenge of the Sith hit theaters - yes, I was the twelve year old the prequels were for, blame them on me, I accept it and I love the goddamn prequels.

I didn't watch the trailer.

I gave Disney Star Wars a fair chance. I watched The Force Awakens in a theater on premiere day. I wasn't impressed. People all around kept telling me that it's just to wipe the slate clean, that from there onwards it'll be a smooth sailing, but I didn't believe it. Still, when the ultra-positive pre-release reviews for The Last Jedi came out (remember the critics telling us it's the best Star Wars movie since Empire, maybe better?), I was cautiously optimistic. In the interim I watched the Rebels cartoon (wasn't impressed) and read some comics and novels (the ones by James Luceno that basically continue from where he left off in the EU were good, but most of the novels are written by new authors and they aren't, you know, good). I went to see TLJ on premiere day. I came out so hollow and empty and no longer being able to call myself a Star Wars fan in a present tense. I didn't go to see The Rise of Skywalker, I didn't give Disney any money for it at all, I pirated the film and only watched it because I took a dare from a friend. 

Then The Mandalorian came out. And The Mandalorian was... a Dave Filoni show. Dave Filoni has a knack for promising a lot of cool stuff and always underdelivering. The Mandalorian wasn't an exception. Instead of a cool space bounty hunter mingling with the Star Wars underworlds we got a Lone Wolf and Cub scenario with a lot of cliches like a Seven Samurai plot rehash - one that Filoni already did in The Clone Wars - but after Season 2 I had hope that they will finally get back on track or that the Boba Fett show will deliver.

You all know how the Boba Fett show went.

I didn't watch that one past episode three. It was so incredibly fucking retarded, I just threw my hands and gave up. There's nothing. This universe is unsalvageable to me.

Did the OT and the Prequels have their flaws? Sure. Did the Expanded Universe have its share of pants-on-head idiotic plots? Of course it did. But it wasn't unsalvageable. It was a mess, but it was a loveable mess.

There ain't anything lovable about Disney Star Wars. It's a corpse of Star Wars. It's like the bug creature from Men in Black wearing decomposing flesh of the man it killed.

After S1E3 of Book of Boba Fett I gave up on watching anything Star Wars.

Bad Batch? Too fucking tired of Filoni's horseshit. Ahsoka? Same. Kenobi? Seen it done better in a EU novel. Acolyte? More like Ain't likely. Andor is actually really good? Happy for it, but I don't give a single goddamn bucktoothed fuck.

I didn't watch the trailer for The Mandalorian and Grogu because I don't care. This isn't my Star Wars. If it's someone else's Star Wars - good for them, they are free to take it. I won't. I won't even support it with my time and attention - and I know the irony of this statement given the fucking novel that preceeded it, but you get my point. I'm done with Disney.

Fuck you, Kathleen. Your best contribution to the art of cinema was being one of the backup dancers in the beginning of The Temple of Doom. Shoulda kept to that, you had some fine legs, but that was your only claim to fame.

- FIRE KATHLEEN KENNEDY…and all the other bad workers ruining Star Wars
- Apologize to the fans for everything
- Decanonize every Disney Star Wars project with the exception of Rogue One, Andor, and the 1st 2 seasons of The Mandalorian
- Recanonize the extended universe
- Make a big animated film based on the extended universe that continues the story after Return of the Jedi
- Continue to adapt the extended universe through movies and shows

replies.text: What years? They DID use EU material for a while now. Sure, it was here and there, some little teasers or easter eggs. They could have not named a planet in Rebels Malachor, but they did. They could have not referenced Revan, but they did. They even used Palpatine returning, which was in EU. Han's and Leia's son turning to dark side is also from EU. Even if they don't adapt EU material directly, I feel it's disingenuous to state that they never used it before. So it doesn't surprise me that they want to use Mara Jade now. They will probably ruin her anyway.

author: @joelt2002
text:
They shouldn't care about "saving" Star Wars, that ship has sailed. They should be looking at a way to salvage the brand. Which means the movie should be a good story with fun elements that Star Wars is known for.

I never thought Mandalorian was any good. It succeeded for a few reasons. One, it wasn't the Sequel Trilogy. So it was a a palette cleansers from that horrific abomination. Two it was post OT but tied into the events of the OT, which is what people craved (also why Rogue One was well received). It didn't try to be epic and gave us a perspective on the Star Wars universe that was unique. As in there was an original story to be told, that people found appealing.

Why wasn't it good? The writing for seasons 1 and 2 (which were the only ones people liked) was safe and hyper generic. It also was loaded with fan service that made no sense for characters within the universe. There is no reason Mando would have intimate knowledge of a backwater like Tatooine.  Yeah the references were "fun" for viewers, but it makes no sense.

I don't think this is setup for a good movie format. Grook works in Guardians because there is an ensemble cast and the other characters talk a lot. Mando does not, Grogu does not.

author: @aszechy
text: I think the fundamental problem is the expectation itself that something like Star Wars can or should have 5000 sequels,  prequels and spinoffs in the first place. What if there just isn't any more in it? Maybe this sounds like sacrilege to superfans, but having recently rewatched the original trilogy I felt that even by the Return of the Jedi, some of it started feeling repetitive, and 3 films were well and good enough. But nowadays we don't really have films anymore - we have "IP", and it's one thing for studios to try to milk them ad infinitum, but it seems fans also expect them to continue forever and they're disappointed when it doesn't work. Of course it doesn't, how could it?
